title: MDM4U Exit Card Learning Package: Linear Regression, Correlation, and Residuals
problemAnchor: When a scatter plot seems to show a relationship, how do we decide whether a linear regression model is strong enough to predict, and how do r, r squared, slope/intercept, residuals, outliers, and extrapolation change that decision?

abt.and: Students have already described scatter plots by direction, strength, and type, including positive or negative linear relationships.
abt.but: A visual trend is not enough: students can mix up r and r squared, misread slope and intercept in context, treat any line as a good model, ignore residual patterns, or extrapolate far beyond the data range.
abt.therefore: The exit card checks whether students can interpret regression statistics and residual evidence to decide whether a linear model is appropriate for prediction.

objective: Students can interpret correlation coefficient r, coefficient of determination r squared, regression line slope and intercept, residuals and residual plots, and can explain why outliers and extrapolation affect prediction reliability.

knowledgeNodes.name: Scatter Plot Correlation Description
knowledgeNodes.definition: Scatter plots can be described by direction, strength, and type: positive or negative direction, weak/moderate/strong strength, and linear or non-linear pattern.

knowledgeNodes.name: Correlation Coefficient r
knowledgeNodes.definition: The correlation coefficient r is between -1 and 1 and describes the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two quantitative variables.

knowledgeNodes.name: Coefficient of Determination
knowledgeNodes.definition: r squared is between 0 and 1 and represents the proportion of variation in y explained by the linear relationship with x. It is positive even when r is negative.

knowledgeNodes.name: Regression Line Interpretation
knowledgeNodes.definition: A regression line y-hat = a + bx predicts y from x. The slope b describes predicted change in y for each one-unit increase in x, and the intercept a is predicted y when x = 0.

knowledgeNodes.name: Residuals and Model Fit
knowledgeNodes.definition: A residual is observed y minus predicted y. Small random residuals support a linear model, while large residuals or a visible pattern in the residual plot suggest the line is a poor or inappropriate model.

knowledgeNodes.name: Least Squares, Outliers, and Extrapolation
knowledgeNodes.definition: Least squares chooses the line that minimizes the sum of squared residuals. Outliers can strongly change the fitted line, and predictions far outside the data range are unreliable extrapolations.

questions.stem: A scatter plot has r = -0.91. What does this tell us?

options.label: A
options.text: There is a strong negative linear correlation.
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct. The negative sign gives the direction, and 0.91 is close to 1, so the strength is strong.

options.label: B
options.text: There is a weak negative linear correlation.
options.feedbackWrong: The direction is negative, but |r| = 0.91 is close to 1, so the relationship is strong, not weak.

options.label: C
options.text: There is a strong positive linear correlation.
options.feedbackWrong: The magnitude is strong, but the negative sign means the trend goes downward.

options.label: D
options.text: About 91% of y is explained by x.
options.feedbackWrong: That interpretation belongs to r squared, not r. r gives direction and strength.

questions.stem: If r = -0.92, then r squared is about 0.85. What does 0.85 mean in context?

options.label: A
options.text: About 85% of the variation in y is explained by the linear relationship with x.
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct. r squared is the proportion of variation in y explained by the linear model.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: r squared does not keep the direction. Direction comes from r, not r squared.

options.label: C
options.text: 85% of the data points are exactly on the line.
options.feedbackWrong: r squared is about explained variation, not the percent of points lying exactly on the regression line.

options.label: D
options.text: The slope of the line is 0.85.
options.feedbackWrong: Slope describes change in y per one unit of x. r squared describes explained variation.

questions.stem: For predicted sales = 4113 - 162.91(price), what does the slope -162.91 mean?

options.label: A
options.text: For each $1 increase in price, predicted sales decrease by about 163 shirts.
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct. The slope is the predicted change in sales for each one-dollar increase in price.

options.label: B
options.text: If the price is $0, the model predicts about 163 shirts sold.
options.feedbackWrong: The price = 0 prediction is the intercept, 4113. The slope is -162.91.

options.label: C
options.text: For each extra shirt sold, price decreases by about $163.
options.feedbackWrong: The equation predicts sales from price, so the slope describes predicted sales change as price changes.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: A correlation coefficient must be between -1 and 1. This number is the slope.

questions.stem: A residual plot forms a clear curved pattern. What is the best conclusion?

options.label: A
options.text: A linear model is probably inappropriate because the relationship may be non-linear.
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct. A visible pattern in residuals means the line is missing structure in the data.

options.label: B
options.text: The linear model is strong because residuals are never useful.
options.feedbackWrong: Residuals are exactly what we use to judge whether the line fits well.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: A curved residual pattern does not mean perfect correlation; it warns that a line may not be appropriate.

options.label: D
options.text: The model is automatically good as long as a calculator gives a line.
options.feedbackWrong: Technology can produce a line for many datasets, but residuals help decide whether that line is a good model.

questions.stem: In the hairdresser tips example, why might a prediction for 32 years of experience be unreliable if the collected data only covered much smaller experience values?
questions.rubric: Strong answer states that 32 years is outside the observed data range, so the prediction is extrapolation; it also explains that outliers can change the regression line and therefore change predicted values.
questions.sampleStrongAnswer: The model was built from smaller experience values, so predicting for 32 years goes outside the data range. That is extrapolation, and the relationship may not continue in the same linear way. Also, an outlier can pull the regression line and change the predicted tips.

diagnosisRules.systemAction: mark passed and continue normal independent practice
diagnosisRules.studentMessage: 你已经能把 regression statistics 和 model fit 连接起来，可以进入正常练习。

diagnosisRules.systemAction: assign reteach modules matching missed errorIds, then retest
diagnosisRules.studentMessage: 你有几个统计解释点还没连稳。先做对应补救，再复测确认。

diagnosisRules.systemAction: start full reteach path, then retest all core errorIds
diagnosisRules.studentMessage: 这节课的 regression 判断框架还没有建立。系统会带你重新理解一遍。

reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: The student reads only the size of r or only the sign, instead of combining direction and strength.

miniLesson.explain: r ranges from -1 to 1. The sign tells direction: positive means upward trend, negative means downward trend. The absolute value tells strength: values close to 1 are strong, close to 0 are weak.
miniLesson.example: In class, r = 0.89 was interpreted as a strong positive correlation, while r = -0.91 would be a strong negative correlation.

guidedPractice.prompt: Identify the concept being tested in one sentence.
Apply the idea to one example from today's regression lesson.
guidedPractice.answer: In class, r = 0.89 was interpreted as a strong positive correlation, while r = -0.91 would be a strong negative correlation.

guidedPractice.prompt: Explain how this idea affects whether a regression prediction should be trusted.
guidedPractice.answer: r ranges from -1 to 1. The sign tells direction: positive means upward trend, negative means downward trend. The absolute value tells strength: values close to 1 are strong, close to 0 are weak.

reteachModules.trigger: Student confuses r squared with r.
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: The student thinks r squared keeps the negative sign or means points are correct/on the line.

miniLesson.explain: r squared is a positive proportion from 0 to 1. It tells how much of the variation in y is explained by the linear relationship with x. It does not tell direction.
miniLesson.example: The lesson used r = -0.92 and r squared = 0.85. That means about 85% of the variation in y can be explained by x, even though the relationship is negative.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: r has direction; r squared has explanation power.

guidedPractice.prompt: Identify the concept being tested in one sentence.
guidedPractice.answer: r has direction; r squared has explanation power.

guidedPractice.prompt: Apply the idea to one example from today's regression lesson.
guidedPractice.answer: The lesson used r = -0.92 and r squared = 0.85. That means about 85% of the variation in y can be explained by x, even though the relationship is negative.

guidedPractice.prompt: Explain how this idea affects whether a regression prediction should be trusted.
guidedPractice.answer: r squared is a positive proportion from 0 to 1. It tells how much of the variation in y is explained by the linear relationship with x. It does not tell direction.

reteachModules.trigger: Student identifies slope/intercept but cannot explain them in context.
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: The student treats regression numbers as abstract values and forgets units, direction, or what x and y represent.

miniLesson.explain: In y-hat = a + bx, a is the predicted y when x = 0. b is the predicted change in y for every one-unit increase in x. Always name the variables and units.
miniLesson.example: For predicted sales = 4113 - 162.91(price), the intercept says predicted sales at $0, and the slope says each $1 price increase predicts about 163 fewer shirts sold.

guidedPractice.prompt: Identify the concept being tested in one sentence.
Apply the idea to one example from today's regression lesson.
guidedPractice.answer: For predicted sales = 4113 - 162.91(price), the intercept says predicted sales at $0, and the slope says each $1 price increase predicts about 163 fewer shirts sold.

guidedPractice.prompt: Explain how this idea affects whether a regression prediction should be trusted.
guidedPractice.answer: In y-hat = a + bx, a is the predicted y when x = 0. b is the predicted change in y for every one-unit increase in x. Always name the variables and units.

reteachModules.trigger: Student ignores a curved residual plot.
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: The student assumes a line of best fit is good just because technology produced one.

miniLesson.explain: Residuals are actual y minus predicted y. A good linear model usually has residuals scattered randomly around 0. A clear pattern, especially a curve, suggests the relationship is not really linear.
miniLesson.example: In the paint additive example, the residual plot curved, so the linear model missed the low-middle-high pattern in drying time.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Random residuals help the line; patterned residuals warn against the line.

guidedPractice.prompt: Identify the concept being tested in one sentence.
guidedPractice.answer: Random residuals help the line; patterned residuals warn against the line.

guidedPractice.prompt: Apply the idea to one example from today's regression lesson.
guidedPractice.answer: In the paint additive example, the residual plot curved, so the linear model missed the low-middle-high pattern in drying time.

guidedPractice.prompt: Explain how this idea affects whether a regression prediction should be trusted.
guidedPractice.answer: Residuals are actual y minus predicted y. A good linear model usually has residuals scattered randomly around 0. A clear pattern, especially a curve, suggests the relationship is not really linear.

reteachModules.trigger: Student trusts predictions even with outliers or far outside the data range.
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: The student does not check whether the prediction is inside the observed data range or whether an outlier changed the line.

miniLesson.explain: Outliers can pull the least-squares line and change predictions. Extrapolation means predicting outside the observed x-values, where the pattern may not continue.
miniLesson.example: In the hairdresser example, the model with an outlier predicted different tips than the model without it. Predicting tips for 32 years was unreliable because 32 years was far outside the collected data range.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Outliers pull; extrapolation stretches too far.

guidedPractice.prompt: Identify the concept being tested in one sentence.
guidedPractice.answer: Outliers pull; extrapolation stretches too far.

guidedPractice.prompt: Apply the idea to one example from today's regression lesson.
guidedPractice.answer: In the hairdresser example, the model with an outlier predicted different tips than the model without it. Predicting tips for 32 years was unreliable because 32 years was far outside the collected data range.

guidedPractice.prompt: Explain how this idea affects whether a regression prediction should be trusted.
guidedPractice.answer: Outliers can pull the least-squares line and change predictions. Extrapolation means predicting outside the observed x-values, where the pattern may not continue.

questions.stem: A scatter plot has r = 0.18. Which interpretation is best?
Explain why r = -0.76 is not the same as r = 0.76.
questions.rubric: Answer must mention same strength magnitude but opposite direction.

retestCards.passRule: 2/2 correct or teacher approves the open response.

questions.stem: If r = 0.63, r squared is about 0.40. What does this mean?
options: About 40% of variation in y is explained by x

questions.stem: Why is r squared never negative?
questions.rubric: Answer must state that r is squared, so negative signs disappear, and r squared is a positive proportion of explained variation.

retestCards.passRule: 2/2 correct or teacher approves the open response.

questions.stem: For predicted pH = 5.43 - 0.053(weeks), what does -0.053 mean?
options: Predicted pH decreases by 0.053 each week

questions.stem: Interpret the intercept 5.43 in the acid rain example.
questions.rubric: Answer must identify 5.43 as predicted pH when weeks = 0, i.e. at the beginning of the study.

retestCards.passRule: 2/2 correct or teacher approves the open response.

questions.stem: Which residual plot supports a linear model best?
Explain why a curved residual pattern is a warning sign.
questions.rubric: Answer must say that the line is missing a non-linear pattern, so linear regression may be inappropriate.

retestCards.passRule: 2/2 correct or teacher approves the open response.

questions.stem: Why can removing an outlier change a regression line?
options: Least squares uses all points, so an unusual point can pull the fitted line

questions.stem: Why is predicting far outside the observed x-range risky?
questions.rubric: Answer must mention extrapolation and that the linear pattern may not continue outside the data range.

retestCards.passRule: 2/2 correct or teacher approves the open response.

teacherEscalation.condition: student fails the same retest twice or scores 0-1 on two consecutive lesson diagnostics
teacherEscalation.message: 该学生需要人工短辅导；问题已经从单个统计术语错误变成 regression model 判断框架没有建立。

nextTrainingIfWeak: r and r squared interpretation sorting drill
